Chapter 15
Gastrointestinal disease
Cathy A. Johnson-Delaney
Anatomy and physiology
Knowledge of the anatomy and physiology of the guinea 
pig gastrointestinal system is essential to the understand-
ing of gastrointestinal diseases. Guinea pigs are herbivo-
rous hindgut fermenters. They are also coprophagic, 
ingesting faeces containing high-fibre particles, as well 
as material from the caecum, directly from the anus a 
number of times per day (Figure 15.1). Obese or pregnant 
guinea pigs may eat faecal pellets left on the floor of their 
enclosure. Young unweaned guinea pigs may consume 
droppings from the sow. If coprophagy is prevented, 
guinea pigs lose weight, digest less fibre and excrete more 
minerals in the faeces.

bacteria are responsible for food breakdown and the pro-
duction of fatty acids (e.g. propionic acid), which are the 
main source of energy for guinea pigs. Dysbiosis (see 
below) can disrupt the breakdown and absorption of nutri-
ents, interfere with peristalsis and result in the production 
of gas and toxins, as well as pain.
The hindgut of the guinea pig involves a system 
whereby bacteria from the caecum are trapped in mucus 
in the colon, with few or no food particles, and are returned 
to the caecum via antiperistalsis. This mechanism appears 
to be less efficient than the particle separation system 
seen in rabbits. In rabbits, there is a furrow located within 
the ascending colon in which the concentration of bacteria 
and nitrogen is twice that of the lumen. Bacteria in the 
proximal colon are transported in the furrow back to the

including inadequate fibre in the diet and any disease that 
causes pain, anorexia and dehydration. It may be related 
to dysbiosis and opportunistic microfloral overgrowth or 
replacement by other pathogenic bacterial populations. 
Differential diagnoses include gastric dilatation and vol-
vulus, intestinal obstruction (due to intussusception) and 
omental torsion.
Radiography is the key to diagnosis (Figure 15.7). A 
contrast study may help to determine whether there is an 
obstruction rather than just hypomotility (Figure 15.8).

Treatment of antibiotic-induced dysbiosis and typhlitis 
is supportive; fluid therapy, analgesia, heat and assistance 
with feeding should be provided as required. Antimicrobial 
therapy can be attempted. The preferred drug for treating 
C. difficile in humans is now vancomycin, although histori-
cally it was metronidazole. Metronidazole has been used 
in guinea pigs, although this may exacerbate toxicosis. 
Jepson (2016) suggests the parenteral use of third-gen-
eration cephalosporins or vancomycin. (Note: no formula-
tion of vancomycin is approved for use in any veterinary 
species, and there is currently little objective information 
regarding the use of vancomycin in veterinary medicine. 
Its use in animals is discouraged due to its role as a last-
resort antibiotic for multi-resistant Gram-positive bacteria 
in humans.) Antibiotic-associated dysbiosis can be pre-
vented by treating guinea pigs with broad-spectrum 
antibiotics if needed for other systemic conditions.

reported survival rate after surgery is <25%. During the

serosa of the stomach to the abdominal wall with a 
continuous pattern. This is usually undertaken when

and gas accumulation can be distinguished from that 
which occurs post mortem by the presence of a haemor-

Rectal prolapse
Rectal prolapse occasionally occurs in association with 
inflammatory conditions of the large intestine or with any 
disease that causes tenesmus or straining. It may also 
occur in young, stressed guinea pigs and can spontane-
ously resolve. Causes include rectal or anal impaction (see 
below), enteritis, colitis and dystocia. It may also occur 
concurrently with vaginal prolapse at parturition. Treat-
ment involves replacement or resection of the prolapsed 
tissue, similar to the approach in other species.
Rectal impaction

anal fold dermatitis and faecal impaction. It typically occurs 
in older, obese boars when faecal matter, along with seba-
ceous secretions, accumulates in the perineal sac so that 
the material appears as a large ‘plug’ at the anal opening 
(Figure 15.15). There will be a strong odour, which is often 
the reason the guinea pig is brought into the veterinary

Intestinal amyloidosis
Amyloidosis is associated with chronic systemic infection 
and/or inflammation. Clinical signs may include malabsorp-
tion and diarrhoea, usually with concurrent weight loss. 
Amyloidosis can be confirmed by identification of amyloid 
deposits in the lamina propria of the duodenum and jeju-
num using histopathology or by post-mortem examination. 
Other organs, such as the kidneys, will also be affected. 
Samples of the amyloid fibrils show apple-green birefrin-
gence under polarized light when stained with Congo red. 
There is no treatment for amyloidosis. It is prevented by 
controlling chronic disease conditions. 15.15 Rectal impaction in an entire boar.

Hepatic lipidosis with ketoacidosis
Severe hepatic lipidosis develops due to anorexia and 
ketosis, resulting in death. Obese boars and periparturient 
sows may be predisposed to this condition. It can develop 
rapidly and significant liver damage may occur within 48 
hours. The guinea pig may present as dull or collapsed, 
dyspnoeic, possibly cyanotic, dehydrated and hypother-
mic. Blood work may show non-specific changes but 
usually reflects a decrease in total protein and the pres-
